comment: Preset-driven front door registry (spec amendment 20c). Patterns ranked and grounded in the 2025-2026 taxonomy research: Menlo Ventures enterprise AI census, a16z 100-CIO survey, LangChain State of Agent Engineering (n=1340), McKinsey State of AI, Anthropic Economic Index, DBTA RAG survey. Every default is an editable assumption; the landing panel makes them loud.

knowledge-rag.label: Internal knowledge assistant (RAG)
knowledge-rag.tagline: Chat over your documents for the whole team or org.
knowledge-rag.howCommon: Top-3 internal pattern; knowledge management is a leading AI function in McKinsey's survey and RAG is the number-two customization technique in Menlo's census.

assumptions.label: 5 queries per active user per day
assumptions.why: Glean-class internal assistants measure about 5 queries per user per day at healthy stickiness.

assumptions.label: 70 percent adoption
assumptions.why: Well-run internal pilots land 60 to 80 percent weekly-active; nobody gets 100.

assumptions.label: 7,000 input tokens per query
assumptions.why: System prompt plus 8 retrieved chunks at ~550 tokens plus the question. The RAG reference scenario shape.

assumptions.label: 8 chunks retrieved
assumptions.why: Production deployments rerank ~30 candidates to a top 5-8. The single biggest cost lever in RAG.

assumptions.label: 40 percent cache hits
assumptions.why: Only the static prompt prefix caches; retrieved chunks miss. Below 40 signals a prompt-structure problem.

assumptions.label: Single assistant, no agents
assumptions.why: Real knowledge assistants are chat-over-corpus, not agent crews. Only 16 percent of enterprise 'agents' are true agents (Menlo).

support-automation.label: Customer support automation
support-automation.tagline: Deflect and resolve tickets with an assistant that escalates to humans.
support-automation.howCommon: The number-one primary agent use case in LangChain's survey (26.5 percent); over 90 percent of a16z's CIOs were testing support AI.

assumptions.label: Volume set by tickets, not seats
assumptions.why: Support runs on interactions per day. The scale step converts your ticket volume into runs; the 'users' figure is the agent-assist pool.

assumptions.label: Router plus worker plus summary, human escalation
assumptions.why: Real deployments are routing workflows with escalation paths, not autonomous swarms (a16z: copilots where 100 percent accuracy is not required).

assumptions.label: 2 tool calls per ticket
assumptions.why: Order lookup and account fetch. Tool payloads are a silent input-token driver.

assumptions.label: 55 percent cache hits
assumptions.why: Stable policies and macros cache well; per-ticket context does not.

assumptions.label: 5 percent retries
assumptions.why: Guardrail rejections and tool failures compound silently at volume.

coding-assistant.label: Coding assistant
coding-assistant.tagline: IDE and CLI assistants for the engineering org.
coding-assistant.howCommon: The largest category in every source: 55 percent of departmental AI spend (Menlo); a third-plus of all Claude traffic (Anthropic Economic Index).

assumptions.label: Split between assistant and agentic use
assumptions.why: Half of developers use AI daily (Menlo); agentic coding burns roughly 15 percent more tokens per hour than assist-mode.

assumptions.label: ~91k tokens per assist hour, ~104k agentic
assumptions.why: Field heuristics for sustained daily use, editable. Cursor-class agentic sessions run hotter.

assumptions.label: 60/40 input/output split
assumptions.why: Code generation is output-heavier than RAG chat; output tokens cost 5 to 6x input.

assumptions.label: 60 percent cache hits
assumptions.why: Repo context and system prompts cache well in modern coding tools.

research-analysis.label: Research and data analysis agent
research-analysis.tagline: Deep-dive research and analysis sessions over data and documents.
research-analysis.howCommon: Number-two agent use case in LangChain's survey (24.4 percent); the one pattern where genuine multi-step agents are real in production.

assumptions.label: 2 deep sessions per analyst per day
assumptions.why: Research is session-based, not high-frequency. Each session is expensive instead.

assumptions.label: True multi-step agent shape
assumptions.why: The one pattern where planner-worker-judge reflects real deployments rather than aspiration.

assumptions.label: 4 tool calls per session
assumptions.why: SQL, code execution, and web fetches. Tool results at ~900 tokens each are a first-order cost here.

document-processing.label: Document processing pipeline
document-processing.tagline: Batch extraction and classification over inbound documents.
document-processing.howCommon: Leading pattern in financial services, insurance, and healthcare back offices (market-sizing directional; the medium example Customer is the verified anchor).

assumptions.label: Volume is documents per day, not chats
assumptions.why: 50 ops staff overseeing 2,000 documents daily. Set this from the real inbound document count.

assumptions.label: 70 percent batch eligible
assumptions.why: Non-interactive pipelines earn the 50 percent batch discount. The biggest single saving in this pattern.

assumptions.label: A judge reviews every determination
assumptions.why: Human-in-the-loop plus machine review is the deployed norm in regulated processing.

sales-copilot.label: Sales and CRM copilot
sales-copilot.tagline: Account research, call prep, and outbound drafting in the CRM.
sales-copilot.howCommon: 87 percent of sales orgs use some AI; embedded CRM copilots are the most common tool (ZoomInfo n=1002).

assumptions.label: 45 percent weekly-active adoption
assumptions.why: 45 percent of sales professionals use AI weekly or more (ZoomInfo). Sales adoption lags engineering.

assumptions.label: CRM and enrichment lookups
assumptions.why: Account and contact fetches drive the integration cost more than the chat does.

it-service-desk.label: IT service desk agent
it-service-desk.tagline: Ticket triage, runbook answers, and routine fixes for IT.
it-service-desk.howCommon: IT Ops is 10 percent of departmental spend (Menlo) and the function where McKinsey finds real agentic deployment leading.

assumptions.label: Tool-using workflow, genuinely agentic
assumptions.why: Service desk is where McKinsey finds agents actually deployed, not just piloted.

assumptions.label: 8 tools exposed
assumptions.why: Ticketing, identity, device management, monitoring. Schema overhead taxes every call.

content-generation.label: Content generation copilot
content-generation.tagline: Drafting, campaign copy, and brand-grounded content for marketing.
content-generation.howCommon: Marketing is 9 percent of departmental spend (Menlo) and a top-reported function in McKinsey's survey.

assumptions.label: 900 output tokens per draft
assumptions.why: Content work is output-heavy, and output is the 5-6x priced side. This pattern's economics invert RAG chat.

team.label: A team (25 to 100 people)
team.why: Licensed seats typically land below the band midpoint before rollout completes. Enter the real seat count.

department.label: A department (100 to 500)
department.why: 240 is a working default for a department, below the 300 midpoint of 100 to 500. Enter the real licensed seat count.

org.label: The whole org (500 to 2,500)
org.why: 900 is a conservative whole-org default, below the 1,500 midpoint of 500 to 2,500. Enter the real seat count.

external-light.label: Customer-facing, moderate volume (about 1,000 interactions a day)
external-light.why: Interaction volume, not seats: about 1,000 interactions a day. Verify against real traffic.

external-heavy.label: Customer-facing, high volume (10,000 plus a day)
external-heavy.why: Interaction volume, not seats: 10,000 plus a day. Verify against real traffic.
